title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in our local awa (stream) - Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional story) of how the stream got its name from the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks. We will connect to Ngāti Kahungunu traditions of water guardianship and the current restoration efforts led by local kaumātua.
learningObjectives: Identify and use 10+ Māori kupu related to water ecosystems and guardianship in spoken and written contexts
Create a pepeha that specifically acknowledges Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and their personal connection to it
Develop and present a simple mihi that expresses kaitiakitanga responsibilities in te reo Māori
Collaborate to create bilingual signage (Māori/English) for the stream restoration project

activities.title: Whakatau: He Pepeha mō Te Awa
activities.description: Students learn the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku from a local kaumātua and create their own pepeha that includes the awa, connecting themselves to this significant local landmark through whakapapa and place.

activities.title: He Kōrero Wai: Kupu Hou
activities.description: Interactive vocabulary building session focusing on water-related kupu (wai, awa, kōawa, repo, kaitiaki) and ecosystem terms through waiata, flash cards, and environmental bingo around the school's wetland area.

activities.title: Te Haerenga: Awa Exploration
activities.description: Guided visit to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaitiaki to observe current restoration efforts, practice new vocabulary in context, and document observations through sketches and Māori phrases.

activities.title: Mahi Toi: Signage Creation
activities.description: Students design and create bilingual educational signs for the awa restoration project, incorporating appropriate Māori phrases, cultural symbols, and environmental messages using both te reo and English.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga: Mihi Presentations
activities.description: Students prepare and deliver a brief mihi to the class explaining their personal kaitiakitanga responsibilities toward Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using the language structures and vocabulary learned.

resources: Kaumātua Hone Smith (local Ngāti Kahungunu elder and stream restoration leader)
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku Restoration Project field guide
Te Papakupu o Te Marautanga o Aotearoa (online Māori dictionary)
Local historical photographs of Mangakōtukutuku stream from Hastings District Council archives

tasks: Completed bilingual signage design with appropriate te reo Māori
Recorded mihi presentation demonstrating kaitiakitanga language
Vocabulary quiz on water and ecosystem kupu
Peer assessment of pepeha accuracy and cultural appropriateness

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori language patterns and vocabulary to communicate about immediate environment
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 4: Recognize how Māori language carries cultural meanings
Social Sciences: Continuity and Change - Level 4: Understand how people pass on and sustain culture and heritage
Health and Physical Education: Healthy Communities and Environments - Level 4: Participate in communal events and contribute to group projects
